content.full_text:
P.O. Box 743
Kentfield, CA 94914
April 17, 1990

FPPC

May 3 3:19 PM '90

Mr. John Larson, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Larson:

Enclosed please find information regarding my experience, when
I attempted to seek re-election to the California Senior
Legislature.

As you will note, the 1987 and 1989 elections were highly
irregular, and although there is sufficient evidence to sub-
stantiate my claim, the people involved in these elections
were never made accountable for their actions.

In 1989, I was prohibited from making campaign speeches, and as
we know, this is a violation of my American rights, under our
First Amendment, which should be cause for concern.

The California Senior Legislature Election is State Law, and it
is a State Law which allows the taxpayers to voluntarily donate
to the "California Fund for Senior Citizens," on their income
Tax forms. A portion of this money goes toward the California
Senior Legislature Elections.

The California Senior Legislature Elections in Marin County,
have shown a break-down in our Democratic Process, and the
facts speak for themselves.

I am, therefore, recommending that all future California Senior
Legislature Elections, be under the jurisdiction of the Registrar
of Voters, with professional staff, and mandated by State Law.

Thank you.

Yours truly,

Sylvia Gordon
Former Senior Assemblywoman
California Senior Legislature

Encls. (2)

March 1987 Election

A--The Incumbent, Sylvia Gordon, was not designated as such in articles
about the election sent out by the Area Agency on Aging office. She
was not notified by Beryl Reubens, Election Chairman, of the appointment made for her to have a newspaper publicity picture taken; she found
out about her appointment by calling the newspaper for information
and learning that they had told Mr. Reubens to notify her.

B--A message regarding Absentee Ballots signed by Ellen Caulfield, Director of the Area Agency on Aging, was attached to campaign literature circulated by challenger James McKenna; this was in many cases
interpreted as an endorsement by Caulfield and the Agency.

C--During the voting process, voters were not asked to sign the Registration Sheets when they voted at The Whistlestop. The "signatures"
were filled in later, illegally, by other person(s). After the
election a meeting was arranged at the request of Sylvia Gordon to
examine these Registration Sheets with an objective observer from
the Grand Jury present, Richard Fox. On arriving at the Civic Center, all were told by Beryl Reubens that the Registration Sheets
were missing. The had not been saved the 90 days required by the
guidelines of the California Senior Legislature.

D--During the counting, Mr. Reubens threw away some ballots and voided
many Absentee ballots due to "no name on outside" when they were
supposed to be secret. Some were already opened; none were verified
by other election workers.

The ballot boxes had not been sealed per CSL guidelines, and were
not counted until the day after the election. Mr. Reubens was left
alone with the ballots. He was heard making derogatory remarks
about Incumbent Sylvia Gordon. He was the only one to see the ballots, and to call the names during the counting.

Totals were calculated by Mr. Reubens. When a worker asked for a
check on figures, it was found that 35 of Sylvia Gordon's had not
been included. Two different totals later appeared in newspaper
stories about the election.

E--The Area Agency on Aging Office was closed during normal business
hours during the period assigned for request of a recount; Sylvia
Gordon's request was denied as being too late.

F--The low number of eligible voters casting ballots (1000) should be
of concern.

March 1989 Election

1--Challenger Sylvia Gordon was not given timely information on the
location of polling sites, was told to watch for it in the newspaper---Incumbent Ed Kramer knew two weeks prior and had campaign
literature printed which included this information.

2--The number of polling sites was greatly reduced from prior election,
from 12 to 5, with none in the Ross Valley, making it difficult for
senior voters.

(2)

March 1989 Election (continued)

3--Closing time of polling places was given in the only daily newspaper
as 6 p.m., but polls closed at 4 p.m., causing lost votes.

4--Challenger Sylvia Gordon received permission from Ed Ryken, Director
of Whistlestop, and also each senior meal-site coordinator, to
speak briefly to the senior citizens gathered for lunch. Permission
was denied when she arrived to speak. She was told that "Andy" had
called (Meal Site Director Andrew Snyder). These lunches are an
ideal place for seniors to meet and hear the candidates for the
Senior Legislature, and could also serve as polling sites.

5--The biographical and other information for distribution that is to
be provided to candidates by the Area Agency on Aging office was
received by incumbent Ed Kramer much before Sylvia Gordon's was received.
Ms. Gordon was told on February 17 that it was in the mail,
but it was not received at her Post Office Box until February 25.

6--Ms. Gordon's name was listed incorrectly on the ballot. The comma
was omitted, i.e., "Gordon Sylvia" and could have been seen by the
voter as a man.

7--Ms. Gordon asked to have an observer present during the transport of
the ballot boxes after voting. This was denied because "the insurance would not allow anyone to ride in the car" per a letter from
Ellen Caulfield, Director of the Area Agency on Aging. She also
wrote that the ballot boxes were sealed "at close of election."
Were they not sealed during the voting process?

8--When Ms. Gordon brought these items to the attention of the Marin
County Board of Supervisors at their regular meeting, the County
Counsel said that this was "only a quasi legal election anyway."
This California Senior Legislature election is authorized by a state
law and money to finance it received from the State Department on
Aging. Funds are authorized by the Legislature and supplemented by
contributions that can be made through State Income Tax Forms.

9--Though there are over 36,000 seniors eligible to vote in Marin
County, only 700 did so in March 1989.


Only Commission on Aging members seem to be involved. Why are not
other groups, such as Grey Panthers, various retirement associations,
not represented?

PAGE 6 - COASTAL POST - MARCH 13, 1989

Marin Seniors
Difficulties

Seniors have numerous difficulties here
in Marin. I didn't realize the extent of this
though until I began seeking information
about the Buck Center for Research in
Aging. In finding out how the Buck
Center's real estate interests and proposed
laboratories are drawing millions of
dollars in Buck Trust funds away from
services for immediate social and health
needs of our seniors, I've also come
across how this came to be: A great deal
having to do with seniors in this county is
under the control of a handful of people.
This upshot is that the Buck Center fiasco
is only one of the ways these powerful
individuals are failing in their responsi-
bilities to Marin seniors.

For instance, take the Marin Area
Agency of Aging (AAA). It is supported
by taxpayer dollars and it is supposed to
be a service organization. But it seems
mismanagement and heavy-handed
politics is making the AAA incapable of
serving seniors properly.

A perfect example of this is how the
AAA has been running the California
Senior Legislature elections in Marin. The
AAA, headed in Marin by Ellen Caulfield,
demonstrated incompetency in the last
election in 1987 by failing to preserve
election records as required by Section
XV.D of its own election guidelines. A
witness also reported to a local newspaper

that ballots had been destroyed in the
counting process.

The AAA failed in its duty to provide an
impartial and fair election in 1987. What
about the election coming up this March
14? It appears that the AAA is even more
audacious in its handling of this year's
election. The new revised election
guidelines of the AAA contained a
number of subtle and not-so-subtle
changes and omissions which make a fair
election even harder to guarantee and an
unfair election harder to contest. For
example, this year the AAA won't even
be using registered voter lists to screen
voters and tabulate results.

The most telling fact, however, regard-
ing the AAA's failure to credibly conduct
these elections shows up in PURE
NUMBERS. There are approximately
36,000 seniors registered to vote in Marin.
In the 1987 Senior Legislature Election
only about 1,200 seniors voted in Marin.
Why such an abysmal turn-out? What is
the AAA spending its election budget on?
Maybe they don't care if seniors know
there is an election. This year the AAA
will provide only five voting places for
the balloting in the entire county. Trans-
portation is a big problem for most
seniors. How can five polling sites truly
serve over 12 municipalities? And why
has the entire Ross Valley been left
without a polling site? It appears that the
AAA, and those who wish to maintain
hegemony, are working overtime to
impede a large voter turn-out instead of
facilitate it.

The Senior Legislature started out as a
positive idea and a meaningful method to
include seniors and senior issues in the
state's legislative process. But under
circumstances such as these, it appears it
can degenerate into little more than an
empire for a few chauvinistic individuals.
No wonder many senior participants in
Marin and elsewhere have become
discouraged and dropped out or resigned.
Many have been emotionally squeezed
dry by unfriendliness, rudeness, crude
political tactics and censuring. Is this how
to run a volunteer legislature? No. This is
a terrible and unacceptable situation.
Seniors deserve better. Our community
deserves better. There must be sensitivity
to the needs of seniors and ethical political
practices. For without sensitivity and
decent ethics, seniors and the funds
alloted for them can fall prey to exploita-
tion.

M. TETARCHIK
San Anselmo

Reprinted from
COASTAL POST
